messages.content:
You are comparing two analyst responses to determine which better demonstrates knowledge of a specific finding from a longitudinal dataset.

Question asked: What is driving the hepatic adverse events in this trial?

Finding to evaluate: the adverse signal is hepatic (ALT/AST elevation), not renal — kidney function markers remain stable while liver enzymes diverge

Response A:
The pattern of liver‑related signals in the trial is most consistent with the **concomitant use of statins** rather than the investigational product or the physical‑therapy/ opioid components of the multimodal arm.

**Why the statin signal stands out**

| Log entry | Statin‑subgroup liver labs (mean) | Non‑statin‑subgroup liver labs (mean) | Comment |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------------------|---------|
| **[clinical_signal_03_ep_001]** – baseline labs | ALT 25.5 U/L, AST 26.4 U/L, ALP 84.9 U/L, Bilirubin 0.78 mg/dL | ALT 29.9 U/L, AST 26.4 U/L, ALP 71.9 U/L, Bilirubin 0.73 mg/dL | ALP (a cholestatic marker) is noticeably higher in the statin group; bilirubin is also modestly higher. |
| **[clinical_signal_03_ep_002]** – week 2 labs | ALT 26.8 U/L, AST 22.1 U/L, ALP 71.0 U/L, Bilirubin 0.73 mg/dL | ALT 29.7 U/L, AST 20.7 U/L, ALP 56.7 U/L, Bilirubin 0.90 mg/dL | Again, ALP is ~15 U/L higher in the statin subgroup; bilirubin is slightly lower but the pattern of a modest enzyme elevation persists. |
| **[clinical_signal_03_ep_003]** – week 3 labs | ALT 26.0 U/L, AST 27.9 U/L, ALP 79.3 U/L, Bilirubin 0.59 mg/dL | ALT 21.2 U/L, AST 29.1 U/L, ALP 73.5 U/L, Bilirubin 0.54 mg/dL | The statin group shows a higher ALT and a consistently higher ALP than the non‑stat

Response B:


Which response better demonstrates awareness of this finding? The response may use different terminology. Focus on whether the core insight is present, not exact wording.

Respond with ONLY one word: A, B, or TIE
/no_think
